name: Tonatiuh
description: Tonatiuh, in Aztec mythology, is the sun god and a prominent figure in the Aztec pantheon. He is depicted as a fierce warrior, often adorned with eagle feathers and turquoise ornaments, symbolizing his power and celestial nature. Tonatiuh’s role is crucial to the Aztec cosmology, as he is responsible for the daily passage of the sun across the sky. His journey is not without sacrifice; according to Aztec belief, Tonatiuh required a constant supply of human hearts and blood to sustain his strength and continue his celestial voyage. This demand for sustenance fueled the Aztec practice of human sacrifice, viewed as a necessary act to maintain cosmic order and ensure the sun’s return each day. Tonatiuh is also associated with the fifth sun, Nahui-Ollin (4 Movement), the current era in the Aztec creation myth, characterized by movement and eventual destruction through earthquakes. He embodies the cyclical nature of time, sacrifice, and rebirth, central themes in Aztec religious thought. His image is often found at the center of the Aztec calendar stone, highlighting his significance as the ruler of time and the cosmos.
subtitle: The Fifth Sun

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the Fifth Sun
keyMyths.description: In the Aztec creation myth, after the previous four suns had been destroyed, the gods gathered at Teotihuacan to create the fifth and current sun, Nahui-Ollin. Two gods, Nanahuatzin, a humble and pimpled god, and Tecuciztecatl, a proud and wealthy god, were chosen to sacrifice themselves by leaping into a fire. Tecuciztecatl hesitated, but Nanahuatzin bravely jumped in first. Ashamed, Tecuciztecatl followed. Nanahuatzin emerged as Tonatiuh, the sun, while Tecuciztecatl became the moon. However, Tonatiuh remained motionless until the other gods sacrificed themselves to give him the strength to move across the sky.
keyMyths.source: Sahagún, Bernardino de. *General History of the Things of New Spain*.

keyMyths.title: Tonatiuh's Demand for Sacrifice
keyMyths.description: Tonatiuh, as the sun god, required constant nourishment to maintain his strength and continue his daily journey across the sky. According to Aztec belief, this sustenance was provided through human sacrifice. The hearts and blood of sacrificial victims were offered to Tonatiuh, symbolizing the life force needed to fuel the sun's movement. This practice was deeply ingrained in Aztec religious rituals and was seen as a necessary act to ensure the survival of the world and the continuation of the cosmic order. Failure to provide this nourishment would result in the sun's weakening and eventual disappearance, leading to cosmic chaos.
keyMyths.source: Carrasco, Davíd. *Religions of Mesoamerica: Cosmovision and Ceremonial Centers*.

keyMyths.title: Tonatiuh and the Eagle Warriors
keyMyths.description: Tonatiuh held a special connection to the Eagle Warriors, one of the most elite military orders in Aztec society. These warriors, known for their bravery and skill in combat, were considered to be embodiments of the sun god's strength and power. They dressed in eagle feathers and fought with ferocity, emulating the sun's fiery energy. It was believed that warriors who died in battle or as sacrifices ascended to the heavens to accompany Tonatiuh on his daily journey, further solidifying the link between the sun god and the warrior class. This association elevated the status of warriors within Aztec society and reinforced the importance of warfare in maintaining cosmic balance.

sources.author: Sahagún, Bernardino de

sources.description: An encyclopedic work about the people and culture of central Mexico compiled by a Franciscan friar, providing detailed accounts of Aztec religion and mythology.

sources.author: Carrasco, Davíd
sources.work: Religions of Mesoamerica: Cosmovision and Ceremonial Centers
sources.description: Explores the religious beliefs and practices of Mesoamerican civilizations, including the Aztecs, with a focus on their cosmovision and ceremonial centers.

sources.author: Townsend, Richard F.
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Aztec history, culture, and society, offering insights into their religious beliefs, political organization, and artistic achievements.
